title: 9 Herbal Creams For Jet Lag
intro_desc: Herbal creams for jet lag are a great way to help your body adjust to a new time zone. You see, when you travel across time zones, your body's internal clock gets confused, leading to symptoms like fatigue, insomnia, and digestive issues. Herbal teas can help relieve jet lag because they contain natural ingredients that promote relaxation and reduce stress. For example, Valeriana officinalis, also known as valerian root, is a natural sedative that helps you fall asleep and stay asleep.  Melissa officinalis, or lemon balm, is another herb that can help calm your mind and body, making it easier to adjust to a new time zone. It's known for its soothing effects and can even help with anxiety and restlessness. Zingiber officinale, or ginger, has anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate digestive issues and nausea, which are common symptoms of jet lag.  Using herbal creams that contain these herbs can bring several benefits to your life. For one, it can help you recover from jet lag faster, so you can enjoy your trip or get back to work sooner. It can also help you relax and reduce stress, making it easier to sleep and focus. Additionally, herbal creams can be a natural and chemical-free alternative to over-the-counter medications, which can have side effects. By using herbal creams, you can take care of your body and mind in a natural way, and get back to your normal routine in no time.

remedies.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis creams contains valerenic acid, isovaleric acid, and valepotriates as its active constituents. These compounds help regulate sleep patterns and promote relaxation, which can be beneficial in overcoming jet lag. Valerenic acid, in particular, has sedative properties that can calm the nervous system and prepare the body for sleep. Isovaleric acid can also help reduce stress and anxiety, making it easier to adapt to a new time zone. By promoting relaxation and regulating sleep patterns, Valeriana officinalis creams can help alleviate symptoms of jet lag and improve the body's ability to adjust to a new environment.
remedies.image_alt: Valeriana officinalis herbal creams for jet lag
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of Valerian root tincture, 2 tablespoons of shea butter, and 2 drops of lavender essential oil.
Melt the coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water.
Once melted, remove the bowl from the heat and stir in the Valerian root tincture, shea butter, and lavender essential oil until well combined.
Pour the mixture into a small container or tin and let it cool until it solidifies.
Apply the Valeriana officinalis cream to your skin as needed to help with jet lag relief and relaxation.

remedies.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis creams contains rosmarinic acid, apigenin, and luteolin as its active constituents. These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help alleviate symptoms of jet lag. The rosmarinic acid in Melissa officinalis creams has been shown to regulate the body's circadian rhythms, while the apigenin and luteolin work to reduce oxidative stress and inflammation caused by travel across time zones. This can lead to improved sleep quality and reduced fatigue, common complaints of people experiencing jet lag. The combination of these properties makes Melissa officinalis creams a useful tool for those looking to adapt to new time zones.
remedies.image_alt: Melissa officinalis herbal creams for jet lag
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Melissa officinalis flowers, 1 cup of coconut oil, and 1/2 cup of beeswax.
Heat the coconut oil in a double boiler until it melts.
Add the beeswax to the melted coconut oil and stir until it's fully incorporated.
Add the dried Melissa officinalis flowers to the mixture and let it steep for 2-3 hours.
Strain the mixture and let it cool. Pour it into a jar and use as a cream to help with jet lag.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale creams contains active constituents like gingerols and shogaols, which are responsible for its potential benefits. These compounds have anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate symptoms associated with jet lag, such as body aches and pains. Gingerols also have antioxidant properties, which can help protect against oxidative stress caused by long-distance travel and changing time zones. Additionally, shogaols have a sedative effect, which can help regulate sleep patterns and promote a good night's sleep, a crucial aspect of recovering from jet lag. By reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation, Zingiber officinale creams may help alleviate some of the symptoms associated with jet lag.
remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal creams for jet lag
remedy_procedure: Mix 2 tablespoons of ginger powder with 1 cup of coconut oil in a small bowl.
Heat the mixture in a double boiler or a microwave-safe bowl for 30 seconds to 1 minute.
Remove the mixture from heat and let it cool for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean bowl.
Pour the cooled and strained mixture into a small container or jar and store it in the refrigerator for up to 2 weeks.

remedies.remedy_desc: Passiflora incarnata creams contains flavonoids, such as luteolin and apigenin, and alkaloids like harmine and harmaline. These compounds help reduce stress and anxiety caused by jet lag. The flavonoids and alkaloids also promote relaxation, which can help regulate sleep patterns disrupted by long-distance travel. Harmine and harmaline can increase serotonin levels, a neurotransmitter that helps regulate sleep-wake cycles. By reducing stress and promoting relaxation, Passiflora incarnata creams may help alleviate symptoms of jet lag.
remedies.image_alt: Passiflora incarnata herbal creams for jet lag
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of dried Passiflora incarnata flowers, 1/2 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil.
Heat coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water until melted.
Add dried Passiflora incarnata flowers to the melted mixture and steep for 10-15 minutes. Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve.
Add vitamin E oil to the mixture and stir well. Pour the mixture into a clean glass jar.
Let the mixture cool and solidify. Once set, your Passiflora incarnata cream is ready to use. Apply a small amount to your skin as needed to help manage jet lag symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia creams contains a high concentration of linalool and linalyl acetate, two key active constituents that contribute to its sleep-promoting properties. These compounds have a calming effect on the body, helping to regulate the sleep-wake cycle and reduce stress levels. The relaxing properties of linalool and linalyl acetate also make it easier to fall asleep and stay asleep, which is particularly useful for people suffering from jet lag. The sedative effects of these constituents can help to reset the body's internal clock, making it easier to adjust to a new time zone. Regular use of Lavandula angustifolia creams may help to regulate the sleep-wake cycle and alleviate symptoms of jet lag.
remedies.image_alt: Lavandula angustifolia herbal creams for jet lag
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/2 cup of shea butter, 2 tablespoons of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of lavender essential oil, and 2 drops of melatonin oil.
Melt coconut oil and shea butter in a double boiler or in the microwave in 10-second increments, stirring between each interval.
Add beeswax to the melted mixture and stir until fully incorporated.
Remove from heat and add lavender essential oil and melatonin oil. Stir well to combine.
Pour the mixture into a container and let it cool and harden. Once set, your Lavandula angustifolia cream is ready to use for jet lag relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Avena sativa creams contains a compound called avenacoside, which is a type of saponin. Saponins have a calming effect on the body and can help to regulate the body's internal clock. Avena sativa also contains a compound called avenanthramides, which has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. These properties can help to reduce stress and promote relaxation, making it easier to adjust to a new time zone. By regulating the body's internal clock and reducing stress, Avena sativa creams may help alleviate symptoms of jet lag.
remedies.image_alt: Avena sativa herbal creams for jet lag
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Avena sativa (oats) and 2 cups of carrier oil like coconut or olive oil.
Steep the dried Avena sativa in the carrier oil in a saucepan over low heat for 2-3 hours, or overnight.
Strain the oil mixture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean bowl, discarding the solids.
Add 10 drops of lavender essential oil and mix well. You can also add other calming oils like chamomile or valerian.
Pour the Avena sativa cream into small jars or containers and store them in the fridge for up to 2 weeks. Use 1-2 tablespoons as needed to help with jet lag.

remedies.remedy_desc: Echinacea purpurea creams contains alkylamides, phenolic acids, and flavonoids as its active constituents. These compounds help to reduce inflammation and promote relaxation, making it easier to adjust to a new sleep schedule. The anti-inflammatory properties of alkylamides and flavonoids also help to regulate the body's internal clock, while phenolic acids aid in the production of melatonin, a hormone that regulates sleep-wake cycles. By reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation, Echinacea purpurea creams can help alleviate symptoms associated with jet lag, such as fatigue and insomnia. The natural compounds in Echinacea purpurea creams work together to support the body's natural sleep-wake cycle and help it adapt to changes in time zones.
remedies.image_alt: Echinacea purpurea herbal creams for jet lag
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of dried Echinacea purpurea flowers, 1/2 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of shea butter, and 2 teaspoons of vitamin E oil.
Melt the coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water.
Add the dried Echinacea purpurea flowers to the melted oil mixture and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean bowl, discarding the solids.
Add the shea butter and vitamin E oil to the mixture and mix until well combined. Pour the mixture into small containers and let it cool before use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba creams contains flavonoids like quercetin and kaempferol, and terpenoids like bilobalide and ginkgolides. These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help protect cells from damage. The flavonoids in Ginkgo biloba creams also improve blood flow to the brain, which can help reduce fatigue and promote better sleep. This can be especially helpful when traveling across time zones, as it can aid in regulating the body's internal clock. The improved blood flow and antioxidant properties may also help alleviate symptoms associated with jet lag, such as headaches and difficulty concentrating.
remedies.image_alt: Ginkgo biloba herbal creams for jet lag
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/2 cup of shea butter, and 1/4 cup of beeswax in a small bowl.
Melt the coconut oil, shea butter, and beeswax in a double boiler or a microwave-safe bowl for 30 seconds, stirring every 10 seconds until melted.
Add 2 tablespoons of Ginkgo biloba extract and 1 teaspoon of vitamin E oil to the melted mixture. Stir well.
Pour the mixture into a small tin or jar. Let it cool and solidify at room temperature.
Once solidified, use a spatula to scoop out the desired amount of cream. Apply to the skin as needed to help alleviate jet lag symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Silybum marianum creams contains active constituents like silymarin, silibinin, and isosilicic acid. These compounds have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that help protect cells from damage caused by free radicals. Silymarin also has liver-protecting properties, which can aid in regulating the body's internal clock and reducing the effects of jet lag. Silibinin has a flavonoid structure that helps regulate the body's circadian rhythms and improve sleep quality. By applying these creams, some people have found relief from jet lag symptoms, such as fatigue and insomnia, due to the creams' ability to regulate their body's internal clock.
remedies.image_alt: Silybum marianum herbal creams for jet lag
remedy_procedure: Get 1 cup of dried Silybum marianum flowers. Grind them into powder using a blender or coffee grinder.
Mix 2 tablespoons of the Silybum marianum powder with 1/2 cup of coconut oil in a bowl. Stir well.
Heat the mixture in a double boiler or a microwave-safe bowl in short intervals of 10 seconds, stirring between each interval, until the oil is warm and the mixture is smooth.
Remove the mixture from heat and let it cool. Strain it through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean bowl.
Store the Silybum marianum cream in an airtight container in the refrigerator. Use it as needed to help alleviate jet lag symptoms.

faq_prevent: Drinking herbal tea may help with jet lag symptoms. Peppermint tea can ease nausea and headaches, while chamomile tea promotes relaxation. Ginger tea is known to aid digestion. These teas can provide some relief, but it's essential to adapt to the new time zone as quickly as possible.
faq_safe: Consuming herbal teas for jet lag every day can be safe if you choose teas with gentle ingredients like chamomile or peppermint. However, be cautious with teas containing strong herbs like valerian or passionflower, as they can cause drowsiness and interact with other medications. Just start with small amounts and see how your body reacts.
faq_timeline: Herbal teas like valerian root and peppermint can help with jet lag symptoms. It's hard to say exactly how long they take to work, but some people notice improvement within a few hours. It might take a day or two for your body to adjust fully, but herbal teas can provide some relief as you settle into a new time zone.
faq_time: Drink herbal tea in the morning, when you first wake up, to help with jet lag. This helps your body adjust to the new time zone. It's especially helpful if you're traveling east, as your body will be trying to wake up earlier. Morning herbal tea can give you a gentle boost.
faq_interaction: Herbal teas might affect how your body responds to jet lag medications. For example, valerian root tea could make sleepiness caused by jet lag medications stronger. Ginseng tea might counteract the fatigue-fighting effects of some jet lag medications. This means herbal teas could change how well your body adjusts to a new time zone.
faq_pregnant: When pregnant, it's best to be cautious with herbal teas, especially for jet lag. Some herbs, like chamomile and peppermint, are usually okay in small amounts, but others, like passionflower and valerian, may affect the baby's development. Stick to well-known herbs like ginger and raspberry leaf, and drink them in moderation.
faq_breastfeeding: If you're breastfeeding and want to try herbal teas for jet lag, be cautious. Some herbs, like peppermint and chamomile, are generally considered safe. However, others like sage and licorice root may affect milk supply or interact with medications. It's best to start with small amounts and monitor your body's response.
title_2: 9 Herbal Creams to Combat Jet Lag for Busy Shift Workers
